data.title: Two 12%+ Yielding BDC Bargains (One Is My Top Deep-Value Pick)
data.description:
Private credit and especially direct lending which is where most BDCs (BIZD) operate is probably one of the last places where durable income investors might be searching opportunities.

If the requirement is to a) not lose money and b) lock in predictable and sustainable (e.g., not funded by NAV) dividends then BDCs might indeed seem speculative.

Based on what we can see in the headlines and on the ground with practical examples such a stance is only logical.

There are many unknowns in the air where each of them can potentially result in notable capital destruction.

SaaS-related defaults, private BDC liquidity outflows, questionable process of fair value marks and potential downturn in the overall credit cycle are just some examples that might trigger bad consequences.

And it is not that the bears have no practical "case studies" to cling to. In fact, they have the "luxury" to pinpoint to, say, two well-established BDCs (with strong brand names) that have recently lost about one third of their market cap levels due to exploding non-accruals.

See the YTD price performance chart of FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK) and BlackRock TCP Capital Corp (TCPC) below:

Now, as I have articulated in my previous articles the BDC land is not as even or similar as it might seem. The differences among managers, strategies and capital allocation tactics can be huge. It is something similar to REITs (VNQ) where it would be incorrect to equate data centers with office REITs, or hotels with industrial REITs.

Just take a look at my Top 3 BDC performance compared to that of the broader BDC market:

This shows that it is possible to insert BDCs into a portfolio without negatively diluting overall income and value durability (as long as the focus is put on high-quality picks).

Yet this kind of approach has two sides:

  1. It brings in a decent BDC yield component without aggressive risks.
  2. It comes with a reduced upside potential which is logical given that there has not been an evident repricing in this particular area (in top quality BDC segment).

Based on my research, I see two huge externally managed BDC bargains which over the past couple of months have plunged into rather deep discount territory.

And what makes the story extra compelling is the presence of robust fundamentals which are strong enough to fulfill the two main conditions for durable income investing: 1) income stability/sustainability and 2) NAV durability.

#1: MSDL, 12.6%

Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL) is a typical externally managed BDC which focuses on core middle market lending. Just as almost all of its peers, MSDL has been subject to quite drastic repricing:

Currently, MSDL trades at a P/NAV of 0.71x which is slightly below the sector average of 0.74x. The discount is significant but what is also important is that MSDL trades below the sector average.

In my view, MSDL has the key fundamentals in a stronger position than for its peers or an average BDC out there.

So, the investment case really has three drivers:

  1. Potential upside from the sector-wide recovery.
  2. Potential upside from MSDL's becoming priced in line with the sector (or very likely trading at a premium to the peers).
  3. Attractive and protected dividend income.

As of Q4 2025, MSDL's portfolio looked like this:

We can see that the portfolio is very diversified across different borrowers and sectors. Granted, the software exposure stands out - just as for most externally managed BDCs - but the quality of underlying fundamentals clearly de-risk the situation.

Here are the most important considerations:

  • 96.2% of MSDL's loan book is tied to first-lien loans which are the safest in the BDC (direct lending) game. The sector average is 77.7%.
  • PIK income accounts for less than 4% of the total income, while the sector average is 8.5%. The PIK for MSDL has remained at these levels in all of the 2025 financial quarters (i.e., it is not a one-off that we see here).
  • The average portfolio company loan-to-value ratio is 40% which indicates that MSDL has not ventured into aggressively leveraged transactions.
  • The non-accrual rate for MSDL is 1.6% which is ~2x below the sector average of 3.5%.

Also, if we look at the recent unrealized losses, MSDL has delivered quite solid statistics. For instance, while in Q4 MSDL did indeed recognize markdowns the consequences to NAV were immaterial - i.e., these markdowns accounted for a bit less than 0.05% of the total NAV.

The NII per share generation (or core earnings) front is rock-solid as well. In Q4, MSDL reported $0.49 per share in NII which was ~$0.01 per share lower than in the previous quarter (mostly driven by lower SOFR headwinds).

Given the recent oil price dynamics and rising inflationary risks, it seems that new base rate cuts are off the table for 2026. So, I wouldn't' expect additional pressure from this aspect.

Moreover, the NII per share definitely benefits from well-structured debt funding portfolio where the lion's share of borrowings is attached to floating rates and the remaining part of fixed rate notes carry somewhat market-aligned yield levels (and the next refinancing event is in Q1 2027).

Finally, it is worth mentioning that recently MSDL revised its dividend downwards due to the pressures that came from lower base rate which compressed the ROE levels. The quarterly base dividend dropped from $0.50 per share to $0.45 per share which still offers an enticing yield of 12.6%.

Because of the realized cut, MSDL's base dividend coverage level has increased to ~109% which is again meaningfully above sector average of 97%. From this level MSDL can retain some part of NII which could be then used for growth funding in a rising spread environment (a timely moment considering how many private BDCs are pulling back their lending activities to handle the liquidity needs).

In a nutshell, MSDL is durable income producer which has a decent margin of safety that comes from three areas: 1) high-quality portfolio (e.g., conservative LTVs, first-lien), 2) surplus dividend coverage and 3) significant discount which sets a floor for the potential downside while providing a notable upside potential from the potential recovery and convergence to sector peer P/NAV levels.

#2: OTF, 12.5%

Blue Owl Technology Finance Corp. (OTF) is for me probably a higher conviction play than MSDL. It has much deeper discount and even higher margin of safety on the fundamental side.

As of today, OTF's P/NAV stands at 0.63x.

This kind of P/NAV is similar to Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN) and PennantPark Investment Corporation (PNNT). The former has registered massive non-accruals and is down ~42% on a TTM basis. The latter has one of the highest leverage levels in the sector and a huge reliance on joint venture income which is where the debt levels are even bigger than what would be allowed per BDC regulation (if these loan investments were on PNNT's balance sheet then the BDC 2:1 leverage ceiling would be breached). PNNT has lost ~30% of its market cap in the past year.

There are multiple reasons behind OTF's deep-value P/NAV:

  • Overall sector headwinds.
  • Ties to Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL) which is OTF's asset manager and Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) which is OTF's sister BDC (both have become subject to notable selloff).
  • Concentration in SaaS.
  • Gradual release of post IPO lock-up shares which flood the market with new supply of shares in a moment when the buying (demand) activity is depressed.

Here is how OTF has performed (price-wise) since its IPO:

However, if we peel back the onion a bit, we will notice how fundamentally sound OTF is and why we could with a reasonable degree of confidence assume that both the dividend and NAV will remain stable going forward.

From the points (negative share price drivers) that I mentioned above, the SaaS or software concentration aspect is the only one which might have some unfavorable impact on the NAV.

This is because of, as the name also suggest, above-average exposure to software - i.e., roughly 70% of OTF's portfolio is linked to software investments.

A couple of things.

First, the data below show that OTF's portfolio investments are spread across different sectors and, importantly, that the underlying portfolio companies are well-established with strong financial metrics. For example, the first-lien loan average EBITDA is $290 million which in combination with average LTV of 34% does not indicate elevated financial risk, to say the least.

Second, the portfolio quality has been almost excellent. In Q4 2025 the non-accrual statistic came in at 0.2% which is one of the lowest levels in the entire BDC space (not only public, but also private).

In fact, in Q4 OTF was one of the few BDCs which reported a net unrealized gain of the portfolio holdings which contributed to a 0.18 per share of NAV appreciation.

Third, OTF carries several investments at the company equity level. One of such investments is associated with Space Exploration Technologies or SpaceX. So, it means that if SpaceX's IPO will be successful then OTF would be in a position to harvest sizeable equity gains that will later feed into NAV growth, strengthening OTF's capacity to fund future growth.

Fourth, speaking of growth funding I have to say that OTF is arguably in one of the best (most comfortable) places relative to other peers. As of today, OTF has the fifth lowest leverage level in the system - i.e., the debt to equity is 0.75x.

Given the usual target capital structure of 1.20x, OTF has a long way to go which is obviously positive and accommodative to NII per share growth.

It must be noted that OTF does not have support from the retained NII as it is for MSDL. Based on Q4 data the dividend coverage was 86% which means that OTF has to utilize debt to close the gap.

However, this is fully logical given the overcapitalized balance sheet. Once OTF will lever the balance sheet up to ~1.20x, the base dividend should be well-covered (as it is also communicated by the management).

And here I'm not factoring in the potential boost from SpaceX IPO nor any tailwinds that might stem from a heightened spread environment (remember, to be a buyer in the current situation when most BDCs are dialing back is a clear opportunity and luxury).

In a nutshell, OTF offers a deep-value investment play which comes with durable dividends, robust fundamentals (high but de-risked SaaS exposure) and gigantic discount.

In my humble opinion, OTF is the best risk-reward case now (among externally managed BDCs and for those who can stomach price volatility).

data.title: TechnipFMC: We Prefer Saipem With More Room To Improve
data.description:
TechnipFMC plc (FTI) has been delivering nicely, with solid margins in both onshore and offshore development businesses. The margin improvements reflect a deliberate profit-first strategy in bidding for projects, as discussed in our previous coverage, that reflects a new phase of margin targets as compared to some of the still liquidating "legacy" backlog. But the thing is that having proven out marginality in these businesses, Saipem (SAPMF), soon to be merged with Subsea 7 (SUBCY), has more space to gain by improving margins, especially onshore. As it happens, Saipem still has an onshore backlog liquidating at bad economics that needs to be digested out. Saipem also trades at a much lower valuation than TechnipFMC despite being in really the same markets, with the industry being basically an oligopoly, particularly now with Saipem and Subsea 7 merging. TechnipFMC loses in the relative value argument. Then, while not the focus of the thesis, we'd also say the run-up in all these stocks' prices may be premature, even though the Iran War has upset oil markets and increased oil prices.

Results

Backlog growth was pretty significant, up 15%. Most of it is, of course, in the much larger subsea division, as opposed to onshore oil engineering and procurement projects. Total revenue was up 9% from last year on backlog growth and execution; sequentially, revenues were seasonally under pressure due to maintenance scheduled in the EoY period.

For the FY, subsea saw 11% growth in adjusted EBITDA, ignoring restructuring expenses, with margins coming up almost above 20%, with the medium-term target being around 21%. The quarterly margin was lower due to seasonality and reflects YoY deceleration as well, though not in terms of profits, with the profit discipline clearly bearing fruit. Continued achievement of EBITDA lift is expected as a result of more new-phase project bidding, where now the company has a more integrated approach and is more disciplined in raising margins and offering clearer delivery timelines, apparently also spawning more greenfield efforts with customers reconsidering previously ignored reserves.

There was less going on onshore, it would seem due to a slower NA market, but profitability is rising along with the subsea segment on better project quoting discipline.

In terms of backlog phasing, it is burgeoning with projects that are expected to see 2026 completion, especially in surface but also significantly in subsea. It is a general rule that later stages of these projects tend to liquidate at higher margins, since the delivery at these later stages takes advantage of infrastructural work, like preparing ports for pipe-laying vessels and things like that, such that incremental costs fall thereafter. This should be a help in raising margins to target, in addition to the fact that new backlog is supposed to be margin accretive.

Bottom Line

But when we note that the surface and subsea margins are really quite similar, and that competing Saipem EBITDA margins for onshore are in the low single digits, we just think that the improvement in the latter is inevitable as it digests out poor backlog, while there is less of a margin of safety in achieving further profit margin growth for FTI, reinforced by the fact that the margins are already reaching near-term targets. The legacy business is being divested out of Saipem. Otherwise, the offshore margins are more similar to each other with FTI, albeit higher. But FTI trades at a 14x EV/EBITDA on a forward basis, while Saipem trades at below 5x. FCFY is also considerably higher at Saipem than at FTI (11% forward vs. 4.62%). Both are beneficiaries of efforts to standardise delivery. Saipem is also likely to benefit from synergies from the Subsea 7 merger, particularly in vessel and other hard asset route economics with the greater density of projects that the combined company will be working on. We think there are more factors that are pushing Saipem up in terms of profitability.

In terms of the war, it has been a reason for a run-up in the stocks of oil services players, but it was delayed until very late March as opposed to the onset of the War.

While ceasefire talks are underway in Islamabad right now, prospects seem limited due to considerable demands from Iran, which nonetheless, through toll threats, puts a high floor on the oil price. That is our view as well. But the run-up in these stocks comes after a settling down of the oil futures curve, which is now forecasting effectively a normalization already by August-September 2026. We may not agree with this optimistic view, which would be to the benefit of oil services companies that will only get mandates if the price of oil seems to be staying sustainably higher, but we don't see the market action as coherent. Also, we aren't sure if these higher prices will prove sustainable enough to spur a step change in activity, even if we suspect that the oil futures curve may be too optimistic. Though factors such as possibly enduring Iranian control and enforcement at the Strait and a higher probability of renewed energy crises could be structural factors that do put upward pressure on energy prices. But opposite factors could be considerable unused OPEC+ supply still in store while phasing out supply cuts, and the geopolitical outcome possible that Iran backs off from the Strait. We don't want to try to predict what will happen.

As far as FTI is concerned, it's relatively overvalued, and there is more of an angle on Saipem.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tesla: From Bye-Bye To Buy-Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
To those that have read my earlier work on Tesla (TSLA), I say that when the evidence points to a change of heart, it would be foolish not to listen and act. The evidence I refer to is multi-fold: recent developments pertaining to volume production of Cybercab, the re-taking of the #1 EV brand accolade from BYD, the removal of a key hurdle to Musk's compensation package, and the Model 2 SUV slated for next year.

Until now, I have lobbied hard against holding TSLA because all the data points led me to that belief, but now that Q1 2026 deliveries are known and the downward trajectory for the EV business has been established, it would be prudent to look at the value that TSLA stock now represents at the current price that is, ironically, 21 percent down from my last Strong Sell article.

At this price, investors can afford to look at TSLA as a buy, even as a speculative position and even without the strong conviction needed to justify such a view. The signs are emerging, and April could well be a pivotal month for the stock and the company. The past has validated my Strong Sell rating. Can the future equally validate my pivot to Buy? We shall soon see. In the meantime, I offer what I believe is a balanced view of the investment narrative around this controversially magnificent stock.

What I Am Bullish About Now?

I shall begin with the positives because there is still a lot going on that could drag the stock down further, but I need readers to focus on these positive aspects regardless. I acknowledge these negatives, some of which still persist, as being the lingering portion of my Strong Sell view until now. However, several new developments have come to light this month (and could come to light later on in the month) that force me to rethink my position.

#1: Giga Texas - The New Center of Tesla's AV Universe?

Musk appears to have kept the promise made in November 2025 to begin Cybercab mass production at Giga Texas. New information has just surfaced two days ago that shows more than 60 Cybercab-like vehicles (remnants of my skepticism, I will admit) were spotted in the "outbound lot" at the factory.

While the report also notes that these vehicles had steering wheels, which Musk originally said they would not (along with side mirrors and pedals), so one can assume these are not the final production units going to market, the fact that manufacturing has begun is, in itself, a major development that could potentially upend bearish narratives, including mine.

If all of this is indeed true - and I defer to the possibly biased but heretofore largely accurate reports from Teslerati contributors - then it implies that Giga Texas could eventually become the "epicenter of Tesla’s autonomous revolution". If this is true, and I believe it will eventually be, then moving its headquarters to the Lone Star State more than four years ahead of this development could, in a sense, be labeled as nothing short of prophetic.

The migration of key Tesla assets to Texas, including legal incorporation in 2024 and the breaking of ground on the Lithium refinery the year before, as well as Boring Company and SpaceX assets already located in Texas, an X unit supposedly beginning Texas operations, and other Texas-based news developments that include the latest one on Cybercab production - all of these are clues to Texas being Ground Zero for Tesla's new ambitions in the realm of robotaxis.

In terms of actual real-world performance, Tesla's L4 and L5 autonomy ambitions may not be as distant as I and many Tesla bears once thought. There are indications that Tesla will achieve a total of 10 billion autonomous miles driven by FSD deployed in its current on-road customer fleet by the middle of this year.

One should bear in mind that the 7 billion miles achieved are NOT unsupervised, and the first run of steering-sporting Cybercabs is proof that L4/5 autonomy might still be several months away from becoming reality, and even longer to become regulation-compliant in U.S. and international markets.

Having said that, it is equally true that the data from these billions of miles are invaluable to the training models to achieve full autonomy. This real-world data puts Tesla far ahead of Waymo, which has racked up "20+ million miles of real world driving and 20+ billion miles in simulation", per its website. One could argue that 20+ billion trumps 10 billion, but those are simulated miles, not real-world. On the other hand, 20+ million miles in real-world situations is about 20+ million miles more than Tesla has achieved, since all of those 7 billion miles done so far have been supervised, and across multiple levels of autonomy.

As things stand now, Cybercab production may be jumping ahead of L4/L5 capabilities, but we know that the hardware is likely already there. According to one report:

It appears that Tesla upgraded the Autopilot computer to a new component, which was first spotted in the parts catalogue on January 21. Soon after that, owners of the new Model Y checked and discovered that the Autopilot Hardware 4.5 computer (or AP45 as it is marked) has been in production since at least December 2025.

This could imply that HW5 is not too far away, despite Musk's usual ambitious target already being delayed by six months or more.

Putting it all together, it would appear that the pieces are gradually falling into place. The promise is slowly becoming reality, and this is essentially what the bulls are betting on with respect to Tesla's robotaxi ambitions. This is the ground floor, just as the Roadster and Model S trajectories were the bottom floors of its EV-led trajectory. The new trajectory will be AV-led, in my opinion.

#2: Current Valuation and Incoming Q1 2026 Earnings Report on April 22

My reason for the bullish pivot is not that TSLA is cheap now. Not by any measure. This year, valuations have dropped by a fifth, but they are still significantly higher than their five-year averages. So it is far from cheap.

Nevertheless, the 'Musk premium' still exists, and this has acted as a floor for the volatile stock, with technicals exhibiting a rising channel.

Even more relevant to my now-bullish thesis is that this premium will continue to exist for the foreseeable future, as will the floor support. And with the Cybercab catalyst now clearly in play, we could see significant gains as the stock bounces off yet another (and higher) support level.

The risk element here is that the stock may break the floor and head lower in the short term, because downward momentum is significant right now. This is also why Q1 2026 results could play a major role in testing that floor.

Analysts are looking for a top-line print of $22.75 billion, which represents a 17.6 percent year-on-year growth rate, and this is despite Q1 deliveries being a known component now. If one looks at the cadence of Street ratings over the past three years, one sees more bullishness weighing in on the stock price.

Indeed, over that period, the Street's consensus price targets have been moving higher, and the stock, although quite volatile around price target levels, seems to be keeping up.

As it stands, these same analysts now have a one-year PT showing 19 percent of potential upside, and I believe the stock will once again keep up despite the short-term risk that I mentioned just now.

#3: The Pivot to Affordability and Autonomy

Tesla seems to have realized that the competition is nipping at its heels in the EV space. Not two days ago, Reuters reported that a "Model 2" SUV pegged at $34,000 may be in the works for next year.

If this is true, it represents a potential catalyst, particularly since Musk signaled on the Q4 earnings call that Models S and X's "honorable discharge because we're really moving into a future that is based on autonomy"

we're seeing, obviously, the first steps along that way this year for Tesla, first major steps as we increase vehicle autonomy and begin to produce Optimus robots at scale.

This is quite possibly the most telling evidence that Tesla is, indeed, marching to a new drumbeat that could eventually rival its peak success in EV sales. Incidentally, Tesla is once again the #1 EV player after BYD sales tanked 25 percent in Q1. If a hard push to bring more affordable EVs to the market materializes, it could effectively stem the declines in production and deliveries.

The strategic shift to autonomy is also evident in how Tesla is aggressively investing in CapEx for Optimus. The winding down of Models S and X is part of a plan to produce up to 1 million Optimus robots a year using erstwhile production space for the outgoing EV models. As for the CapEx outlay, per CFO Taneja:

And at the moment, we are expecting that CapEx would be in excess of $20 billion. We'll be paying for 6 factories namely, the refinery, LFP factories, Cybercab, Semi, a new Megafactory, the Optimus factory. On top of it, we'll also be spending money for building our AI compute infrastructure, and we'll continue investing in our existing factories to build more capacity. And then also the related infrastructure along with it. And we'll also further expand our fleet of Robotaxi and Optimus. While this may seem a lot, we believe this is the right strategy to position the company for the next era, and we'll make such investments, as Elon mentioned, in a very capital-efficient manner.

The 2.5x CapEx jump from $8.5 billion in FY 2025 to "in excess of $20 billion" may not be fully covered by operating cash flows ($14.7 billion in FY 2025), but Tesla has ample short-term liquidity exceeding $44 billion that can fuel this ramp.

There will obviously be significant pressure from the declining bottom line over the past three years, but I see this level of CapEx as realistic over the next year or two, after which robotaxi and Optimus revenues should ramp up to start strengthening the bottom line and operating cash flows again.

In Conclusion

My shift from Strong Sell to Buy is obviously a major one, but it is based on the intensity of recent news flows, as well as key developments that have led to this point.

I do not presume to tell you that the stock is going to go up from here because I do see some short-term price weakness, but my thesis is a more long-term one that envelops Tesla's strategic shift from being an EV-heavy entity to one that is autonomy-heavy.

I think we are once again on the ground floor of an exciting journey ahead.

data.title: Western Midstream: A 9% Yield That Still Grows In A Downturn
data.description:
Western Midstream (WES) actively manages costs, capital intensity, and contract structures to ensure that its financials stay as agnostic to throughput variations as possible. Operating data demonstrates a very stable EBITDA range of ~$540-610m through periods of volatility in volumes and earnings. While that is not a sufficient reason for a buy on growth terms alone, that becomes a very strong anchor for income investors.

The combined returns from a ~9% yield and ~5% embedded EBITDA growth mean investors can look at early double-digit returns as a base case. And that through a valuation regime that has also remained within a defined band in the recent past. The buy thesis on income grounds does not rely on further rerating and should be able to accommodate ~10% multiple compression - that is if Western Midstream tests the valuation lows seen in the past couple of years. The core buy call relies on Western Midstream's positioning as a relatively cycle-agnostic play - something that looks structurally supported, repeatable, and proven through weak environments.

EBITDA Stability

Since the core of the thesis is a relatively stable EBITDA, it is important to assess the degree of stability compared to the volatility in net income over the past 5 years (as shown in the chart below). In fact, in 2025, EBITDA almost defied a sharp drop in net income - that has often been achieved through a set of very actively managed levers.

The GAAP EBITDA numbers show a dip in Q4 2025 (charts above), but adjusted EBITDA was reported at ~$636m. If accounting noise in adjusted EBITDA were removed, due to ~$30m of negative revenue recognition adjustments in the quarter, the numbers would have been up sequentially by ~5%. This is demonstrated EBITDA resilience through a period of volume and net income pressures. Even in Q4, there were temporary throughput curtailments due to negative gas pricing at Waha (producers chose to shut volumes rather than sell at uneconomic prices).

The guidance for 2026 is an EBITDA of ~$2.5-$2.7b, aiming for a ~5% EBITDA growth off a normalized base. This is in line with management's aim of long term average EBITDA growth in the mid-to-low single digits, despite an outlook for flat gas volumes and oil/NGL volumes down by low-to-mid single digits. That 2026 volume outlook may not change materially due to the Iran conflict and higher oil prices immediately, but it does improve Permian drilling economics (if the effects of the conflict are sustained). That means the environment becomes supportive for higher activity and associated gas over time. Budgets and infrastructure limits mean response is constrained and lagged, and importantly, it needs confirmation of a sustained higher energy price regime (and US strategic energy asset emphasis). I do not factor in any upside due to the energy prices variable (impact is indirect anyway), but I do think the crisis provides a factor that could reduce further volume contraction.

Western Midstream typically keeps EBITDA stability intact through measures that look structural and repeatable. Cost reductions are a lever that is actively used. The operation and maintenance expenses are down by ~12% YoY, through a series of optimizations that are expected to continue in 2026. That includes measures like fewer contractors, better sourcing, and facility redesign. These changes appear to be process improvements that should persist (not temporary cuts). Additionally, Western Midstream has cut capex by ~$200m (deferring low priority expansions). G&A expenses are also projected to stay flat despite integration activity (Aris) - balanced by optimization projects in IT systems, corporate efficiencies, etc. On the revenue side, protection due to fee-based and MVC structures means Western Midstream gets paid per unit or minimum volume regardless of actual throughput. All in all, EBITDA volatility is well managed and is expected to stay the same way, despite volume volatility.

Self-Funding Transition Mechanics

How does the stable EBITDA story translate to cash flow realities? The 2026 guided EBITDA of ~$2.6b should translate to ~$1.85-2.05b distributable cash flow (DCF) according to management estimates. Leaving aside ~$925m for capex (midpoint of the ~$0.85-$1b guidance), we still have ~$1.025b of post-capex cash generation - that is good internal funding capacity. Now, at ~$3.7 distribution per share for the ~393m shares outstanding, the distribution commitment adds up to ~$1.45b. That is a gap of ~$0.4-$0.45b against the post-capex cash, broadly using midpoint estimates (could change a bit depending on actual EBITDA, capex, etc.).

I am not worried about the earnings power because with ~$2.6b of EBITDA converting into ~$1.85-2.05b of DCF, the business is already generating significant cash before capital allocation decisions. The more relevant question is around capital intensity.

Current capex is limited in Pathfinder and North Loving II primarily, and these are already contracted and can be expected to contribute from 2027. So, over the course of a few quarters, we will see a double boost to the post-capex cash as capex rolls off and EBITDA expands. That gap of ~$0.4-$0.45b we are seeing today could be easily closed due to these effects, pulling Western Midstream into a self-funded regime. This transition is irrespective of how volumes move or commodity prices shift - this is a mechanical improvement from assets that are contracted and can be monetized.

As of today, too, that post-capex gap is not a cause of concern - I brought that up to show how post-capex cash is set to grow stronger, not to address issues in Western Midstream's buy thesis. Net debt today is ~$8b (with an EBITDA of ~$2.5-2.6b), implying leverage of ~3.1x, which is at the lower end of the sector. The strong balance sheet bridges the post-capex cash gap today seamlessly while enabling capex flexibility. Plus, Western Midstream has shown how it can use capex spending as a tool to stabilize capital allocation.

A Solid Income Play

The growth expectations with Western Midstream are as predictable as they can get, largely due to the company's emphasis on EBITDA stability. With an EBITDA growth of low to mid single digits and a yield of ~9% (with low single-digit distribution growth very much possible, given historical dividend growth trends).

Once Pathfinder and North Loving II are commissioned and start adding incremental fee-based volumes, EBITDA grows further while capex normalizes. Water segment expansion (Aris integration) increases exposure to produced water handling, which is structurally growing faster than hydrocarbons and is typically fee-based with long-term contracts. This adds a more stable and higher visibility revenue stream. Cost efficiencies are also lasting and structural.

Importantly, EBITDA and distribution stability are not dependent fully on volumes or higher drilling activity. That makes Western Midstream a highly predictable incremental compounding vehicle in my view. That is why I find it a Strong Buy - not as a high growth or cyclical rebound play, but as a reliable income play.

Investors should watch three things for the buy thesis to break: sustained deterioration in EBITDA, capex not rolling off over the next year or so, and any loss of cost discipline. EBITDA can only structurally decline by 10-15% (levels that may warrant a review of the Buy call) if volume weakness moves beyond what contracts and cost control can absorb. That would require a more extreme setup than today's already weak backdrop - producers cutting activity sharply, MVC protections rolling off, and underutilization across assets.

data.title: After A Chaotic Q1, I'm Buying XLK And XLC As The Market Exhales
data.description:
Thesis

The first quarter of 2026 was a really bad time to trust first impressions. Investors got bounced from one narrative to the next: war, oil, rates, AI, and earnings, often all at once. I called it a “bipolar” stretch: we’d end the day down, then wake up and reset to hope.

Not surprisingly, energy stocks were the top performers in the first quarter. Meanwhile, Technology and Communication Services stocks as a whole lost value. Basically, investor fear had a bigger influence on which sectors did well than clear economic signals.

At the same time, company earnings overall didn’t fall apart. Per FactSet Earnings Insights:

For Q1 2026, the estimated (year-over-year) earnings growth rate for the S&P 500 is 13.2%. If 13.2% is the actual growth rate for the quarter, it will mark the sixth-straight quarter of double-digit (year-over-year) earnings growth reported by the index.

My research argues that this is now a sector-selection market, not a market for blindly owning what held up best during the panic. The situation now suggests that investors should focus more on parts of the market that dropped earlier in the first quarter because of fear, but still have solid and believable earnings to support them. In parallel, they should be more cautious with sectors that have already benefited from rising oil prices or from investors moving into safer, defensive investments, since those gains may already be priced in.

The Case for Technology

Despite the rocky performance of tech stocks during the first quarter, it's clear that tech remains a great investment based on what's happening behind the scenes: companies are making continued large capital outlays. Gartner projects a rise in worldwide IT expenditures from approximately $6.15 trillion this year, a rise of 10.8% from 2025. In addition to overall IT expenditures, AI expenditure specifically is projected to reach $2.52 trillion, reflecting a year-over-year increase of 44%. This cements that AI's not merely a passing fad but a vigorously developing industry, and firms are more than happy to continue to invest significant sums into this area.

This all lines up well for the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK), but only if you really understand what’s inside it. XLK is basically all tech (99.91%), holds 77 stocks, and is essentially top-heavy: 61.38% of its money is in just its top 10 holdings. For example, Nvidia (NVDA) makes up 15.50%, Apple (AAPL) is 13.63%, Microsoft (MSFT) is 10.05%, and Broadcom (AVG) is 5.37%. And when you add in names like Micron Technology (MU), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Applied Materials (AMAT) , and Lam Research (LRCX), it’s fairly obvious it’s heavily focused on big tech and especially semiconductors and infrastructure. My point is, that concentration directly links XLK’s performance to the health of the semiconductor cycle. According to the Semiconductor Industry Association, global semiconductor sales in February 2026 jumped 61.8% compared to last year and 7.6% from January. That pretty much kills the idea demand’s cooling off.

There’s another layer to this story too. That kind of growth points to sustained end-market demand, particularly from hyperscalers scaling out AI infrastructure. Amazon (AMZN) is seeing massive growth in AI through AWS. Reuters reported that its AI services business is now running at over $15 billion in annual revenue, and Amazon expects to spend around $200 billion in 2026 on capital investments, mostly for AI infrastructure. That circles back to XLK and why hardware, like chips, servers, memory, and networking gear, is still such a big deal. Spending in those areas hasn’t slowed down at all; if anything, it’s still ramping up.

Taken together, none of this points to a weakening sector, so the recent underperformance needs to be explained elsewhere. I think that the Q1 weakness in tech had more to do with a one-two combo punch between, quite frankly, this Middle East fiasco leading to WW3 talk and valuation pressure than broken fundamentals. Oil spiked big time, inflation fears picked back up, and the market started pushing out the path for Fed cuts, which is usually enough to hit a concentrated growth sector like XLK.

At the same time, what usually gets sold first? The best of the best, so it wasn't crazy to blame investors for selling some great performers as they bought into the idea that AI optimism was already priced in after a huge run. Importantly, that interpretation is now being echoed by major Wall Street firms. As reported by Reuters earlier in the week, Wells Fargo recently upgraded its rating on technology to favorable. The reason for this move is that, despite the hand-wringing, the sector’s core strength hasn’t really cracked. In addition, Goldman Sachs noted that the relative underperformance of the Technology sector has now created a buying opportunity for investors, due to earnings expectations remaining well above their historical average. So the key issue here is not that technology suddenly lost its growth engine. It is that the market became less willing to pay peak multiples for that growth during a quarter dominated by oil shock, war risk, rate and societal anxiety.

That said, XLK isn’t exactly cheap, and SA's data shows it does come with some risk: 21.55% annualized volatility, 18.16 standard deviation, and a C+ risk grade. On the flip side, it’s very efficient and easy to trade, with an A+ expense grade, a super low 0.08% fee, about $90.77 billion in assets, and strong liquidity.

The Case for Communication Services

Communication Services is actually in better shape than it looked after the first quarter. When you think "communication services," especially if you've been in this game for a while, it's easy to have flashbacks that it's just made up of old-fashioned telecom by the large telcos. This is no longer the correct assumption. The newer, more current drivers of this sector include digital advertising, streaming, and platform monetization, all of which continue to grow nicely.

For example, the Interactive Advertising Bureau’s 2026 outlook says U.S. ad spending is expected to grow 9.5% this year. Digital is leading that growth again, with social media up 14.6%, connected TV up 13.8%, and commerce media up 12.1%.

That lines up well with the Communication Services Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLC). XLC is 99.77% invested in stocks and holds just 27 companies. It’s very top-heavy: 71.69% of its assets are in its top 10 holdings. Meta Platforms (META) makes up 18.20%, Alphabet Class A (GOOGL) is 10.65%, Alphabet Class C (GOOG) is 8.54%, and Netflix (NFLX) is 5.91%. Then you’ve got companies like AT&T (T), Verizon (VZ), T-Mobile (TMUS), Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), Electronic Arts (EA), and, of course, Walt Disney (DIS).

XLC is made up of companies from advertising platforms, streaming services, telecommunications, and media. Because of that, when advertising activity and user engagement increase or decrease, XLC tends to move in the same direction. There’s also another important factor to consider. The same AI spending that’s boosting the Technology sector is also helping the big platform companies inside XLC.

You might recall last year's forecast that blew past analyst estimates for the third quarter for Meta Platforms because its AI tools were getting better at targeting ads. So the ads got smarter, the costs got higher, but revenue kept moving anyway.

Also reported in February was that Alphabet expects to spend between $175 billion and $185 billion in 2026 on capital expenditures. At the same time, Google Cloud revenue growth reached 48%. So Alphabet keeps shoveling money into AI and infrastructure, and paired with strong cloud numbers, it’s a sign that demand is still there.

Streaming is helping as well. Based on what came out in late March, Netflix raised prices across its U.S. plans, including the ad-supported tier. Analysts expect that to increase average revenue per user in the U.S. and Canada by about 6% in 2026. Since Netflix is a big piece of XLC, it’s worth noting not all growth comes from social ads, some companies can still charge more and get away with it.

Putting it all together, this doesn’t look like a sector that’s falling apart. Nothing fundamentally broke in Q1, the sector just got swept up in the market’s usual habit of dumping everything at once because of fear, and throwing the proverbial baby out with the bathwater in the process.

Keep in mind, XLC's very concentrated, so funds like that tend to feel valuation pressure faster. The trade-offs are clear in the data: XLC has a D+ risk grade, 14.27% annualized volatility, and 71.69% of its assets in its top 10 holdings. On the flip side, it has an A+ expense grade, a very low 0.08% fee, about $24.74 billion in assets, and strong liquidity.

Final Takeaway

At this time, the markets appear to want one thing over anything else: less madness. While I write this, we are in what almost every media source has described as a "fragile" ceasefire and with that the markets reacted perfectly. Stocks shot up, oil dropped sharply and you could feel it was two parts short covering and one part a large relief sigh. I don't need perfection here, nor do I usually, but I would prefer to have less of the bipolar tape we saw last month and more of last year's possibilities: stability, peace, growth and clearer earnings paths. That's how it all feels to me right now. Last year seemed like nostalgia compared to this train wreck. The downside is that a fragile ceasefire is still exactly that: fragile. Flow rates of oil remain under pressure, the Strait of Hormuz is still not back to normal, and the elevated cost of energy continues to impact inflation pressures. You and I both know that increased oil price support is not limited just to the energy sector; eventually, a share of those increases will drip, drip, drip their way into consumer products and household pockets which will create a level of risk against my bullish thesis.

For now, I still prefer to be in the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund and the Communication Services Select Sector SPDR Fund, but I feel more confident about one than the other. I consider XLK a “Strong Buy” because companies are still spending heavily in this sector, especially on AI infrastructure and semiconductors.

But I rate XLC a “Buy,” but for slightly different reasons. This sector depends more on consumers and advertising spending. Companies like Netflix can raise prices, and platforms can keep finding ways to make more money, but at the end of the day, people can choose to cut back and save money. So while there is still good potential for growth, it’s more sensitive to pressure on household budgets, especially if oil prices stay high and inflation continues to hurt spending.

data.title: S&P 500: A Dead Money Era May Be Here, How To Thrive In It
data.description:
Last month, something happened that apparently did not get much headline attention. I noticed it, because I'm a technician. That means I look for patterns in the data that could be meaningful, especially if they draw my attention to risks not widely acknowledged by the markets. That's because above even my focus on technicals/charts/data trends, I'm a risk manager.

What happened? The S&P 500's 5-year rolling return ducked under its 15-year return. Big deal, so what? Well, it might be a very big deal. I'll show you why, using some highlighted pictures, in a moment. First, let's set the scene at what increasingly looks like a major market pivot point to me.

Here are three things you should immediately understand about what this article intends to say:

  1. Over the next 2-3 months, I have no idea what the stock market will do. Sometimes I have a stronger feeling than at other times. But investing is not about feelings, it is about results. And in my career, the best results have come, not from being "right" but by not being "bigly wrong" in a way that creates drawdowns that take quarters or years to recover from. Because recovering from a steep drawdown costs me time. I don't like wasting time.
  2. The most important time frame to me is not the next few weeks or the next many years. Those are too short term and too long term. Too short, and you get fooled by the "madness of the crowd" and "animal spirits." Too long and you leave yourself wide open to your capital being underwater for a very long time. Not often, but it happens.

    I kn    ow that memories can be very short in this business. So here's a 12-year (yes, I said "year") period in which the S&P 500 produced a return of 1.21%. So about 0.1% per year without compounding. This is what I mean. Markets are inherently cyclical.

I have not stopped looking for return. But I am operating under the view that the risk of major loss is abnormally, historically high. In fact, there could be a lot of return to gain by profiting from falling markets or segments thereof.

There are two types of "major loss." The 2025, 2022, 2020 style, which recover quickly. And the 2000-2013 style, which create "lost decades." I do not know if we are on the verge of another lost decade. I've invested through two of them in my career. However, I think the chances of that are higher than they've been since that last one ended in early 2013.

Lost Decade?

This is a strong phrase. 10 years with no return on stocks? I can't say with anything close to certainty that this is what is ahead. However, I can size up the odds. And I'll start doing that now. Because while there is an "equity culture" featuring "buy every dip" and FOMO and all of that, historically that is the chapter the precedes the start of something that reverses that era. That's when lost decades occur.

This is not about predicting. It is about preparing. For a wider range of possible outcomes than we are used to. Including an extended period where the popular stock market indexes are "dead money" for years.

We've been in an era of "how much will the S&P 500 go up this year." While much of the market has been in a years-long slumber, obscured by the headline indexes' success.

How abnormal have these past many years been? Here we see the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) versus another, very different, Invesco product, their Russell 1000 Equal Weight ETF (EQAL). That's a market whose headlines do not match the broader trend.

This goes back to late 2014, EQAL's inception. That's a more than 3:1 gain, QQQ over EQAL. That is one of many macro headwinds I think will characterize the next 1-3 years. And that is the time frame most relevant to me as an investor.

This chart prompted me to write this article

What is that above? It shows annualized 5, 10 and 15-year returns for the S&P 500. I'm glad I looked it up, because when you have been doing this as long as I have, some things just stick in your mind from past market crises. And while there's a hyper-focus on "the last" 5, 10 or 15 years, that is as deceiving as any performance data point I can provide to you here.

The 5, 10 and 15-year S&P 500 returns are all clustered in the 10-13% range. That's great. But historically, it tends to lead to much weaker returns in the subsequent periods. In particular, when those returns start to come down off their highs. And that is exactly what is starting to happen. So while the market is currently in a point of stasis, the "math" of this history, shown above back to 1970, is screaming at me, "pay attention!" When we see that cluster of returns, it usually means that we've pulled forward so much future return that the market has very little left in the tank.

When did this also occur? 1973, 2000, and 2008.

Dates worth remembering. Because that's when the three most damaging bear markets of the past 60 years occurred.

This chart includes my yellow highlights of when the 5-year annualized S&P 500 return "crossed under from above" the 15-year annualized return. Is it an exact science? No. Nothing is in investing. The takeaway is less detailed.

You see, by the time the S&P 500 has come off its lofty 5-year return peak, the market is often already starting to fade. This is really just a different application of technical analysis, which I am all about.

To me, this is merely a big warning shot. Something to be aware of as the market just seems too happy despite a long list of challenges. And frankly, as I detailed in my last article, most stocks do not look good beyond a trader's time frame.

Here's a close up view of the period from the start of 2024 to now. You can see what might be the final crest of these rolling returns. This looks more and more like the downside of the mountain to me.

This is the most important chart in this article

Now, let's isolate those periods (this century) where the 5-year return ducked below the 15-year return. I've highlighted those in yellow.

Here's what you see above:

  • Dot-com bubble: the break occurred during summer 2001
  • Global financial crisis: late May of 2008
  • 2020 Covid: early March, but it didn't last long and quickly recovered
  • 2022 Rising Rates: a few close calls, but didn't stay broken for long
  • March 17, 2026: it happened again three weeks ago. Something that happened only about six times since 1970. It just happened.

That puts us in the "watch carefully zone." I know I am doing exactly that.

What am I doing about this? My own approach has been technically-based since last century. So that's not changing. But it is evolving as we speak. I've written here briefly over the past couple of years about how I've taken 40+ years of charting stocks and funds by hand, then visually, and more recently converted it into a single indicator. I now run that ROAR (Return Opportunity And Risk) Score for any stock or ETF, any day. It essentially reads the chart the way I taught it to read it.

And true to form, as shown here, it has been, shall we say, "sanguine" on the S&P 500 index for the past six months. Much of my written work here and elsewhere has documented this.

But ROAR, my direct/live chart reading, and my investing approach more broadly is NOT focused on "buy or sell" signals. My concern with anything I produce as research is not to guess up or down. Like I said earlier, up is always possible, for any stock or ETF at any time. What varies over time is how much risk is attached to that pursuit of return.

Of the many model portfolios I run, this is the "flagship." All it does is take the ROAR Score of SPY each week (it was 40 as of this writing, on a scale where 0 is higher risk, 100 is lower risk). I invest that percentage of one of my accounts in SPY, and the rest is invested in BIL (T-bill ETF). Every week, the model follows the ROAR Score.

Here's that same past six months in traditional technical analysis format. This nets out to a "nothing burger." However, the longer this goes on, and the market has a lid on it, the more likely it is that those longer-term (5, 10, 15-year) rolling returns for the S&P 500 continue to bleed lower. I'm just connecting the math between short-term and long-term time frames here.

This is why my portfolio is focused on a range of managed risk strategies. From bond ladder, to low-correlation ETFs including inverse ETFs, to above-average cash position, and some use of options as a surrogate for equity exposure. Case in point, when volatility recedes, I am more likely to buy out of the money S&P 500 call options, or even a small position in a leveraged S&P 500 or Nasdaq 100 ETF.

Embracing volatility while managing risk

The goal for me, always, is not to run from volatility. But to take account of the cyclical big picture, and manage that market I have. Not the one I'd prefer to have. We don't get those choices.

The cyclical nature of the S&P 500 is indicating more strongly all the time that multi-year stock market returns have peaked. That means it is time to expand the "toolbox" and prioritize risk management.

data.title: Vistance Networks Looks Better Than Before With Powerful Earnings Growth
data.description:
Vistance Networks (VISN), formerly CommScope, is right in the middle of a big reset right now. The company sold off its Home, OWN, DAS, and CCS businesses. Now what’s left is mainly the Aurora and Ruckus segments. Both of them did really well in 2025. Aurora brought in $1,232.7 million in sales, Ruckus hit $698.9 million. If you look at adjusted EBITDA, Aurora jumped 137.6% from last year, Ruckus even more at 306%. That’s $251.9 million for Aurora, $127.5 million for Ruckus. Add it up, and company-wide adjusted EBITDA hit $292 million. Last year it was just $24.5 million. The numbers are way better now, and the company looks a lot leaner.

The last time I wrote about VISN, the stock was around $18.24, and I called it a buy. Since then, it’s mostly remained flat, now trading at $18.73. They’ve had strong quarters back to back, including a Q3 beat and a strong Q4 report.

The CCS business was sold to Amphenol in January 2026 for $10.5 billion. That deal mattered: it paid down all the big loans and got rid of the preferred equity. Now they’re sitting on $923 million in cash, and management said all debt was extinguished after the deal closed. With the balance sheet unlevered and excess cash in hand, VISN has announced a $10 special cash dividend, which supports the idea that the CCS sale did more than reduce risk; it also created immediate value for shareholders.

Aurora And Ruckus

Aurora’s momentum right now depends closely on how fast DOCSIS 4.0 gets rolled out. Management said they’re shipping more DOCSIS 4.0 amplifiers than ever, and they already landed a big DOCSIS 4.0 contract with a major North American cable operator. That left their year-end backlog at $631.8 million. More shipments are lined up for the first half of 2026. On the Ruckus side, they’re in the middle of a longer upgrade cycle as companies move over to Wi-Fi 7. They’ve scored deals with several big U.S. professional sports stadiums. There’s a lineup of announced Wi-Fi 7 contracts, which gives some clarity on where the revenue’s coming from, and they’ve put more money into their sales team and incentives to try to hang on to these gains while enterprise networking is still early in its shift to the next generation.

Margin Improvement

The company’s numbers improved a lot, mostly because of higher volumes, with Ruckus also benefiting from pricing, and you can see it in EBITDA margins. Aurora’s margin went from 12.7% in 2024 to 20.4% in 2025. Ruckus moved up from 5.7% to 18.2%. When you add them together, the core adjusted EBITDA for the combined business hits $379.4 million at a 19.6% margin. Margins got better mainly because of higher volumes, while the company’s strategy stayed focused on growth areas like DOCSIS 4.0, PON, and Wi-Fi 7.

Valuation

VISN’s valuation looks easier to understand now that management has laid out a clear 2026 adjusted EBITDA range of $350 million to $400 million. At the current share price of $18.73, the stock already reflects a lot of recovery, but not necessarily all of the benefits from a debt-free balance sheet, excess cash, and a business centered on two better-performing segments. If the company lands around the middle of that EBITDA range and margins stay close to current levels, a fair-value range in the mid-to-high $20s still looks reasonable.

Demand Cycles

Demand for this stuff has improved a lot, thanks to big capital spending cycles in cable and networking. VISN’s main markets are right in the middle of major upgrades: DOCSIS 4.0, fiber-to-the-home rollouts, and new Wi-Fi 7 gear. These aren’t quick, one-and-done projects; they take years. VISN’s problem isn’t that their products don’t fit or aren’t different enough. They’ve still got good relationships with the big cable customers, and Comcast accounted for about 35% of 2025 net sales. The real issue is that infrastructure spending comes in waves, mostly dictated by the customers’ own schedules. Competition is tough in both of their main businesses, and the company itself flags competition as an ongoing risk. But VISN’s technical know-how and all the equipment they already have in the field give them some protection that’s hard for new players to break through.

Risk Factors

Growth in its segments could get derailed if demand drops off or if there’s some kind of bigger economic problem. Stuff like rising input costs, trouble getting parts or workers, tariffs, currency swings, and depending too much on a few customers, especially Comcast—these aren’t just hypotheticals. Any of them could cause real problems down the line. Even with the cleaned-up balance sheet, VISN could take another hit to goodwill if one of its divisions sees earnings fall off. If they’re slow to turn backlog into sales or if a big customer suddenly cuts orders, confidence could slip fast. The stock’s valuation would probably drop with it. If margins don’t hold up, EBITDA could fall back pretty quickly.

2026 Outlook

The outlook looks solid, but there are conditions. The company says it’s aiming for $350-$400 million in adjusted EBITDA by 2026, but management has made clear that execution in DOCSIS 4.0 and Wi-Fi 7 will matter a lot to any future gains. Key things to watch: Aurora keeping EBITDA margins above 20% in its segments, Ruckus growing revenue by double digits, and turning backlog into revenue fast. How these play out will basically prove or disprove the main argument for owning the stock over the next year or so.

Buy Rating

I’m rating VISN a buy. The risk is high, but not out of control. The main businesses are getting things done, and the new, simpler setup means they can focus and decide where to put money more easily. The market they’re in still looks good. Still, I’m not calling it a strong buy. The company has a lot riding on a few customers, and the usual risks from market cycles are still there. If they can’t turn backlog into sales, if EBITDA margins drop, or if there’s a big, surprise write-down at Aurora, I’d have to change my view. Until then, VISN looks like it could be a decent way to grow your money over the next few years, not just as a bounce-back play, but as the start of a stronger, more valuable business with a real shot at higher long-term returns and some flexibility for the future.

data.title: ZIM's $35 Buyout: Why The Market Is Wrong To Doubt Hapag Lloyd's Winning Bid (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've been covering ZIM Integrated Shipping Services Ltd. (ZIM) during the last year. In my last article, I explained why I believe that the Hapag-Lloyd will go through as it complies with the Israel Golden share clause. Despite this, the stock is trading at a substantial discount to the $35 offer, and after reviewing the 6-K form, I consider the downside even lower, especially since Maersk was also willing to pay $31 per share. Moreover, rates have been surging and are above 2025 levels, which should provide another profitable year for the company. Given the current discount offering a 35% upside to Hapag Lloyd offer and the confidence that it will close, I upgrade the stock to strong buy.

Background

ZIM Integrated Shipping Services is an Israeli liner company with a light business model that relies on chartering in most of its ships.

We don’t have updated presentation as the company suspended the Q4 call due to the Hapag buyout offer. However, in the Q4 press release indicated that in March the current fleet consisted of 115 containerships, this implies that the company has renewed the three vessels up for renewal in 2025.

From those 115 containerships, the company only owns 16 while the others are leased. However, the newbuilds that represent 60% of the fleet are leased on very long term charters (with buy options) which is similar to owning the vessels.

Eleven of the owned vessels will be transferred to the New Zim under the takeover offer as is one of the requirements of the Israeli Golden Share. I recommend reading the “Background of the Merger” section from the 6-K form as it provides much colour about how much both time the companies spend to structure the deal to comply with the Golden Share.

I expect the government will approve the current transaction, since even Netanyahu suggested this solution when ZIM faced bankruptcy:

The idea of such a split is not new. It was first proposed in 2003 by Benjamin Netanyahu, then serving as finance minister, who suggested dividing Zim into two companies, one focused on global operations and the other on maritime transport to and from Israel, with the golden share attached. Although the proposal was approved, it was never implemented, and the state’s holdings were eventually sold to the Ofer family.

However, even if Hapag Lloyd’s offer is rejected and the company can’t resolve the issues through negotiations with Israel, ZIM disclosed that Maersk made a bid for $31 per share.

Moreover, rates have been moving up and are above 2025 levels, suggesting there could be even more interest in acquiring the company.

Financial Position and Valuation

As mentioned in previous articles, ZIM has a strong financial position with $2.8 billion in cash (about $23.2 per share) and $5.7 billion in lease liabilities. However, this is not traditional debt; rather, it represents the charters for its ships. In fact, the interest in acquiring the company is mainly due to those charters, which means they are more of an asset than a liability.

The strong financial position together with the assets the company owns and the charters below market rates, led Hapag Lloyd to offer the mentioned $35 per share, far above my previous estimation about $25 to $30 per share.

The assets that ZIM owns (the 16 containerships, containers boxes and buildings) are valued at $1.3 billion on the balance sheet ($7 billion from the green box in the balance sheet image above, minus $5.7 billion corresponding to the right of use assets, which is the same as the “debt”).

Most of this value is from container boxes that are very liquid and in total those assets represent almost $11 per share. Moreover, as mentioned before, ZIM also has $23.2 in cash per share giving a total of $34 per share on liquid assets, almost matching the Hapag Lloyd’s offer. I was expecting some discount due to the Golden Share and the long term market prospects; however, it seems that Hapag Lloyd valued more the long term charters at attractive rates.

The $35 per share cash offer provides an attractive 34% return at the current stock price.

Risks

The main risk is that the Israeli government will veto the operation. While I don’t expect that, I believe that before forbidding it, they will try to negotiate and given the recent strength in tanker rates, Hapag-Lloyd has some margin to accommodate Israel demands.

Even if the Israeli government vetoes the operation for other reasons, such as Saudi and Qatar minority interest in Hapag Lloyd, and given Maersk’s interest to also acquire ZIM, I expect them to replicate the deal structure and close the deal.

If no deal is successful, then I expect a sharp stock decline.

Conclusion

ZIM is trading at an important discount to the $35 Hapag Lloyd offer. This is due to the uncertainty regarding the Israeli Golden Share; however, the 6-K form provides great detail on how both companies studied the case and are confident the deal will go through. It also reveals the existence of another offer at $31 per share.

I am confident that the deal will close, and I consider the current discount very attractive, offering almost a 35% upside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Don't Overlook Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. When Investing
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

Do you love your insurance?

For most of us, insurance is the protection we have in the case of a situation we never want to find ourselves in. It's one of those items that you need to have in those moments, but for the most part, it's an expense we have to accept because those moments rarely come.

Because insurance is a massive sector that few people really want to spend time digging into or thinking about, it allows opportunities to persist.

Today, let's take a look at a company we've covered previously and review its earnings to see if the opportunity persists.

Let's dive in!

Harvesting Mega-Deal Merger Synergies

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. (AJG) does business in 130 countries and recorded $13.8 billion in revenue in 2025. AJG is a dominant force in the global insurance brokerage industry.

The company is divided into the Brokerage and Risk Management segments. The former advises on insurance programs and places coverage for clients. Accounting for 88.4% of the total 2025 revenue, it is the company's engine. Comprising the remaining 11.6% of 2025 revenue, the latter segment provides third-party claims administration and consulting.

AJG closed out 2025 with a solid fourth quarter. The company's total revenue climbed 33.9% higher year-over-year to $3.57 billion in the quarter, which was its 20th consecutive quarter of double-digit top-line growth. That roughly matched the analyst consensus during the quarter.

This was mostly due to the transformative acquisition of AssuredPartners, with over 30 additional tuck-in mergers driving additional growth. The remaining growth was 5% organic revenue growth.

AJG's adjusted EPS increased by 10.2% year-over-year to $2.38 for the fourth quarter. This was due to the natural lag between the AssuredPartners acquisition and fully realizing the operational efficiencies.

The good news moving forward is that the Chairman and CEO J. Gallagher Jr., noted in his opening remarks during the Q4 2025 Earnings Call that the back-office integration is ahead of plan. That means AJG remains on track to realize $160 million of annualized synergies in 2026, with an additional $100 million to $120 million expected by early 2028.

Meanwhile, the company continues to incrementally add revenue through tuck-in acquisitions. AJG can leverage its low cost of capital with its BBB+ S&P credit rating to keep these bolt-on deals flowing.

That's why we agree with the FAST Graphs analyst consensus for 15.2% annual adjusted EPS growth through 2028, off a 2025 base of $10.69.

AJG's 1.3% dividend yield is also well covered. The payout ratio is positioned to be in the low-20% range for 2026. This should help it to build on its 15-year dividend growth streak with at least high single-digit percentage annual dividend growth (for a Chowder number of around 9 or 10).

AJG is a deep value pick. At the current $220 share price (April 8th, closing price), shares are priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 16.7. According to data from FAST Graphs, this is considerably under the 10-year average P/E ratio of 24.

Looking out over the next several years, we would argue that a sensible fair value P/E ratio for AJG is around 22. The forward annual adjusted EPS growth consensus of 15.2% is fully intact against the 10-year CAGR. We believe that this standard deviation of difference from the 10-year average multiple prices reflects much of the risk facing the company. Our fair value per share estimate of $299 implies the stock is currently trading at a 29% discount.

If AJG meets growth projections and reverts to fair value, it could log a 50% total return through December 2027. By the end of 2031, the insurance broker could put up 18% annual total returns.

Pertinent risks to AJG include the integration risk of its mega-deal and the potential for AI disintermediation.

The company's acquisition of AssuredPartners is the biggest in its history. If it fails to integrate the company into the mix/can't reach the $160 million in 2026 synergies (it is currently ahead of schedule), that could act as an overhang on a reversion to our fair value target.

This manifested with the launch of a new AI tool by Insurify in February. The concern is that AI could automate the middleman roles in insurance, which could weigh on AJG's profit margins. The mitigating factor is that the company is fairly insulated from this for the foreseeable future because its focus is primarily on the upper middle market (e.g., complex commercial, wholesale and specialty, risk management insurance, and reinsurance). These require a level of subjectivity that AI hasn't yet reached and likely won't for quite some time, which buys AJG time to adapt.

Conclusion

AJG has been extremely successful at integrating new insurance companies into its family while completing its massive acquisition of Assured Partners. Even as recently as a couple of days ago, AJG was announcing yet another successful buyout of an insurance brokerage firm to add to its family of companies. At a recent conference, AJG's CFO and Corporate VP indicated that they are not chasing multiples on M&A. That being said, they're saying that if they have excess cash, they're going to be using it to buy back their shares, especially at these prices. This will help provide a floor to how far AJG can fall as the company buys back shares and removes them from the overall market flow. Overall, AJG is an extremely healthy company with an attractive outlook.

Insurance is a complex and, at times, confusing realm to try to invest in. There are many different avenues in which you can invest in the insurance sector, whether in brokerages or companies that offer their insurance directly, or in reinsurance companies that help reduce the risk of primary insurers. I find AJG to be an extremely attractive route to gain exposure to this sector without the risks created by other avenues.

Don't pile on unnecessary risk in your hunt for strong performance. A fundamentals-first and data-driven investor is willing to be patient as sentiment clears to be rewarded with strong returns.

data.title: Global Ship Lease: Embedded Upside From Charter Repricing And Trade Disruption
data.description:
After a year of our coverage initiation (Combining Value and Growth) and a total return above 100%, we are back to comment on Global Ship Lease (GSL). Since our last update (Q3 analysis), the company reported impressive Q4 results in early March, but we expect GSL to benefit over the longer term from ongoing disruptions in global trade flows.

As a reminder, despite an impressive share price performance, we have maintained a high-conviction positive supported by 1) strong operational delivery, 2) rapid deleveraging, and 3) a clearly shareholder-focused capital return policy. Yet, the stock continues to trade at an undemanding valuation. In our last analysis, we also identified incremental upside from regulatory changes in the U.S. that limit Chinese leasing competition, as well as higher secondhand vessel prices, both of which enhance the underlying asset base.

Fig 1

Why are we still positive?

Firstly, Global Ship Lease released its Q4 and FY 2025 results during the Middle East crisis (Fig. 2). Here at the Lab, we believe GSL will be a clear beneficiary of this escalation. The disruption in the Middle East has triggered a sharp increase in Time Charter Equivalent (TCE) rates across shipping segments. We are talking about oil and gas carriers, but even container charter rates are firming due to vessel shortages and higher tonne-mile demand. According to a Freightos update, there is a 5-10% price hike in TCE. Additionally, another impact is the vessel shortage. This is due to longer rerouting, port delays, and risk constraints. This has reduced available fleet capacity, tightening the charter market (and again) will lead to higher TCE rates. GSL’s earnings are highly sensitive to charter rate increases; however, the company has 99% contracted vessels in 2026 and 81% in 2027. Therefore, ~19% of the fleet still needs to be repriced for next year. This means that GSL has no upside on EBITDA uplift for this year. However, the company trades at a depressed valuation, with an EV/EBITDA of 4x or less and an asset value of its vessels in operation ($1.9 billion) below its current market cap ($1.4 billion).

Fig 2

Secondly, and still related to our evidence, last year we reported how “Liberation Day” tariff announcements would create volatility in trade. In a challenging time, we believe top-tier container liner companies will secure GSL service. This is due to a longer wait for newbuild deliveries and the flexibility to avoid committing investment CAPEX to purchasing a ship. The asset-light approach among GSL’s customers is set to persist, and these recurring distribution events create a virtuous cycle for the company.

In GSL history, the company's leverage has always been a headwind. This was due not only to GSL's valuation but also to a lower per-day break-even rate. The TCE per day price is a ratio that calculates daily revenue performance by "subtracting voyage expenses from voyage revenue and dividing by the total voyage duration." In the expenses, interests are included. The company's net debt reached $695 million (Fig. 3), with EBITDA of $521.4 million. This is very supportive and will be a clear benefit in the future.

Fig 3

Earnings Changes And Valuation

Last time, we forecasted an average 2026 rate of $30k per TCE per day. This resulted in a turnover of $730 million with an EBITDA of $535 million. Considering the latest vessels in operation, the company has already reported higher sales in 2025, with $766.5 million in results. Considering no additional investments, and 99% of sales covered, GSL's top-line sales might reach $820 million. We should report that the company has limited OPEX and minimal SG&A. Applying an EBITDA margin (aligned with 2025 results) of 68%, we reached a margin of $557.6 million. Including interest payment ($20 million), and D&A of $120 million (there is limited CAPEX guidance aligned with historical average and adjusted for annualized inflation - Fig 4), our net income reached $417.6 million. As a reminder, GSL is a Bermuda-resident company and does not pay corporate tax. Assuming no buyback, GSL's current shares outstanding are 35.92 million, so we derive an EPS of $11.6. Continuing to apply a 5-year P/E average of 3.6x, our new target price should be $41.8, representing a 9% capital appreciation opportunity. This is a company that has purchased $150 million in new vessels per year (2/3 units) and has demonstrated strong execution and a deleverage story. Last time, we included a statement that we continue to see fair, and we reiterate today, "We don't want to play with a higher target P/E, but we believe it is well justified, so we leave it to our readers to decide. We still maintain our long position."

Fig 4

Risks And Threats

GSL's downside risks include trade disruptions that could trigger force majeure among its clients. The company might be subject to a slowdown in the container shipping industry's growth. Regarding the company, we see risks from an aging fleet and higher maintenance costs due to CAPEX constraints. Additionally, we see risks in client concentration (Technomar) and in higher regulatory requirements. The company is subject to negative global events. The Strait of Hormuz has a limited impact on GSL; however, potential disruptions to shipping routes, such as the Suez Canal, could affect the company's P&L.

Conclusion

All in all, we believe the company will continue to deliver and, sooner rather than later, be recognized by Wall Street. Even if GSL has limited earnings upside, the company has secured contracts for the next few years, and this should be reflected in the valuation. Therefore, we remain buyers.
